Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine this semester. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but then I'd just feel this heaviness and couldn't bring myself to start. When I did study, I preferred doing things with my hands—making flashcards, rewriting notes—but even that felt exhausting most days. I'd often sit with my materials open but just stare at them without really absorbing anything.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I wouldn't touch anything, and then I'd panic near a deadline and try to cram everything in. I know I should have been more consistent, but between my anxiety and just feeling sad all the time, keeping a regular schedule felt impossible. The only predictable thing was that I'd always be behind.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd realize something was due or that I'd missed a class, and then I'd log in to see how bad the damage was. Sometimes I'd get notifications on my phone and feel this wave of dread before clicking through. It was rarely because I actually wanted to learn something—more like checking how much I'd messed up.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, logging in every morning with my coffee. But that fell apart quickly when I started feeling overwhelmed. The LMS just became this thing that reminded me of everything I wasn't doing, so I avoided it more than I engaged with it.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful when I actually watched them. I could pause and rewind, which helped because my mind wanders a lot. Sometimes the discussion posts were interesting too—seeing other students' perspectives made me feel less alone. But honestly, I didn't use most things as much as I should have.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the readings and the practice quizzes. The readings felt endless and I couldn't focus long enough to get through them on screen. I kept telling myself I'd print them out and highlight them, but I never did. The quizzes stressed me out because I was afraid of seeing how little I actually knew.
Q7: Something felt worth my time if I could actually do something with it, not just passively read or watch. Like if there was a hands-on component or if I could discuss it with someone. But most things just felt like boxes to check, and when you're already feeling low, checking boxes doesn't feel meaningful at all.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. I'd think, 'well, it's optional, so I don't have to do it,' and that gave me permission to skip them. Required ones at least had the pressure of consequences, but even then I'd often not complete them. The optional stuff just disappeared into the background noise of everything else I was avoiding.
Q9: Yes, definitely. I downloaded a lot of materials early in the semester with good intentions. I have notebooks full of handwritten notes from when I was trying to learn by writing things out physically. I also watched some YouTube videos on topics I didn't understand. None of that would show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were times I'd spend hours with my printed notes spread out on my bed, trying to make sense of concepts by drawing diagrams or talking through them out loud. Or I'd be in the library with a textbook, not the course materials. My brain works better when I'm moving or touching things, so a lot of my actual learning happened away from screens.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a PDF or save a video, watch or read it once to know what it contained, and then work with it offline for days. I highlighted printed pages, made physical flashcards, stuck things on my wall. The LMS would just show one download or one view, but that material might have been my focus for a whole week.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart. I was already anxious, and then assignments started piling up. I felt paralyzed—like I couldn't even open my laptop without my chest tightening. I started missing more classes, which made me more anxious about going back, which made me miss more. It was this awful spiral.
Q13: Absolutely. I was juggling seven online courses, which was way too much. The cognitive load was crushing. When I was exhausted or confused, I'd just close the LMS tab because looking at it made me feel worse. Some weeks I had other responsibilities—family stuff, trying to take care of myself—and the courses just couldn't compete.
Q14: So many times. I'd open a lecture video and let it play while I was completely zoned out, thinking about other things or just feeling numb. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. I was technically 'there' but not present at all. It was like going through the motions without any real connection.
Q15: I postponed almost everything important. I knew the assignments mattered—my assignment scores were actually decent when I did them—but starting felt impossible. The anxiety would build up so much that avoiding the task felt like the only way to breathe. I'd tell myself 'tomorrow' every single day until suddenly it was too late.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that got me moving, but in a really unhealthy way. I'd ignore everything until the last possible moment, then scramble in a panic. Sometimes I'd miss deadlines entirely because I'd frozen up. The LMS would probably show these bursts of activity right before due dates, which is pretty embarrassing to admit.
Q17: I barely looked at feedback, honestly. When I did, it either made me feel worse about myself or I'd already moved on mentally. Positive feedback didn't really register because I was so focused on everything I was failing at. It's like the feedback existed in a separate universe from where my head was at.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming. Seven courses, all with their own schedules and expectations—I couldn't keep track. There wasn't enough flexibility for someone like me who needs to learn by doing rather than just reading or watching. The rigid deadlines didn't account for the fact that some weeks I could barely function.
Q19: The lack of hands-on activities made it harder. I'm a kinesthetic learner, and everything was reading and videos and typing. If there had been more interactive elements, simulations, or practical applications, I might have stayed more engaged. Also, the sheer volume of content in each module was intimidating and made me shut down.
Q20: I think that's exactly what happened to me. I'd log in, click around, watch videos, read posts—but then when it came to actually submitting work, I'd freeze. The anxiety of being judged on my work was worse than just passively consuming content. Activity doesn't equal productivity when your mental state is blocking you from following through.
Q21: I can see how that would happen for some people. If you're a good test-taker or you study effectively offline, your LMS activity wouldn't reflect your actual learning. I wish that was my story, but for me, low activity meant low engagement overall. Though my assignment scores were decent when I actually submitted—so maybe there's something to that.
Q22: A course log can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how long you spend. But it completely misses the quality of that time. It can't tell if you're actually learning or just staring at a screen feeling empty. It also misses all the offline work, the emotional struggles, the reasons behind the numbers. It's just a shadow of the real picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it. It's wanting to understand, not just pass. For me, engagement would mean showing up mentally and emotionally, not just physically clicking through materials. This semester, I wasn't engaged—I was just surviving, barely.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling with anxiety and sadness. It wouldn't show the nights I cried because I felt like a failure, or the mindfulness exercises I tried to calm myself down enough to study. It wouldn't show that I actually care about learning—I do—but that something inside me was broken this semester. The data just shows a drifter, but there's a whole person behind those numbers who was drowning.
